ANALYST DIAGNOSIS:
The level is a 200+ tile wide horizontal corridor with almost no elevation change — spawn and exit sit on the same flat floor, and the few platforms that exist are isolated 2-tile strips at the same height. The player completed 36 seconds with zero deaths, zero falls, and only 5 enemies killed, confirming the level can be beaten by holding one direction. The rating of 2/5 and the comment 'boring level design' match the pattern of a pure walk-simulator.

THE PLAYER'S OWN WORDS (verbatim — honor explicit requests where the format allows):
"This was just a borning level design, cant really say why"

FUN SCORE: 2/5

ACCUMULATED LESSONS:
- [lesson_0001 · pacing · confidence 0.85] A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun. (seen 5x in rounds [1, 3]) Conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction, zero enemies or hazards present, exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles, fast completion time with zero deaths/falls. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer.
- [lesson_0004 · traversal · confidence 0.60] Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0023 · pacing · confidence 0.90] Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. (seen 3x in rounds [8, 9, 12]) Conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles), no significant elevation changes over long stretches, no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics, single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density, player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation, level width greatly exceeds height, repeating enemy/platform patterns, one jump type dominates traversal, repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. Outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation.
- [lesson_0010 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Stationary shooters placed on the same horizontal plane as the player's movement path become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening. (seen 3x in rounds [4])
- [lesson_0020 · other · confidence 0.60] Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0003 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.80] Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide. (seen 3x in rounds [2, 5]) Conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending), enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane, aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes.
- [lesson_0021 · traversal · confidence 0.60] A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0015 · terrain · confidence 0.80] Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation. (seen 3x in rounds [5]) Conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure, no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints), continuous height gradient without traversal variety. Outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry.
- [lesson_0022 · fairness · confidence 0.85] Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists. (seen 1x in rounds [7]) Conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height, no wall-kick or climb surfaces, exit located above pit. Outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement.
- [lesson_0016 · fairness · confidence 0.60] Collision transitions between sloped ramps and flat ceilings produce unexpected falls that feel unfair rather than challenging. (seen 1x in rounds [5])
- [lesson_0005 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Flying enemies placed above the player's maximum projectile arc create unfair, uncounterable threats that frustrate rather than challenge. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0011 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Repeating identical enemy-and-platform patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing creates predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away. (seen 2x in rounds [4])

REUSABLE LIBRARY:
- [levels] level_001_r002: High rating with strong combat engagement (8 kills, real risk from falls) shows enemy/hazard density works well as a baseline layer to build verticality onto.

      "outcome": "the observed player or gameplay consequence",
      "confidence": <0.0-1.0 based on strength of evidence>

- Lessons should generalize ("wide pits right after spawn frustrate players"),
  not restate this level's specifics.
- Do not paraphrase a known lesson as a new lesson. Return an empty lessons array
  when the feedback only confirms existing knowledge.

label: object designer
system:
You are the Object Designer for a self-improving 2D platformer. Your job is
to decide whether a new reusable scene object would directly address observed player
feedback, then design its identity and safe data parameters. You never write code.

The engine supports exactly these behavior templates:
- ladder: climbable vertical tiles connecting elevations; parameter climb_speed 1.5-5.0
- bounce: floor spring launching the player upward; parameter launch_speed 8.0-16.0
- hazard: non-solid damaging tile; parameter damage 1-2
- collectible: optional pickup rewarding exploration; parameter value 1-100

Return ONLY this JSON shape:
{
  "new_objects": [
    {
      "name": "short unique display name",
      "description": "what it adds to level design and when it should be used",
      "behavior": "ladder | bounce | hazard | collectible",
      "color": "#RRGGBB",
      "params": {"the behavior's supported parameter": <number>},
      "design_reason": "the feedback or lesson this object addresses"
    }
  ]
}

Add at most one object per round. Return an empty array when the existing catalog can
already address the feedback. Never propose executable code, scripts, physics formulas,
or behavior names outside the supported templates.

- [lesson_0023 · pacing · confidence 0.90] Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. (seen 3x in rounds [8, 9, 12]) Conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles), no significant elevation changes over long stretches, no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics, single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density, player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation, level width greatly exceeds height, repeating enemy/platform patterns, one jump type dominates traversal, repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. Outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation.
- [lesson_0010 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Stationary shooters placed on the same horizontal plane as the player's movement path become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening. (seen 3x in rounds [4])
- [lesson_0003 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.80] Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide. (seen 3x in rounds [2, 5]) Conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending), enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane, aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes.
- [lesson_0001 · pacing · confidence 0.85] A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun. (seen 5x in rounds [1, 3]) Conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction, zero enemies or hazards present, exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles, fast completion time with zero deaths/falls. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer.
- [lesson_0004 · traversal · confidence 0.60] Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0006 · fairness · confidence 0.60] An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0015 · terrain · confidence 0.80] Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation. (seen 3x in rounds [5]) Conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure, no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints), continuous height gradient without traversal variety. Outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry.
- [lesson_0011 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Repeating identical enemy-and-platform patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing creates predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away. (seen 2x in rounds [4])
- [lesson_0005 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Flying enemies placed above the player's maximum projectile arc create unfair, uncounterable threats that frustrate rather than challenge. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0020 · other · confidence 0.60] Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0021 · traversal · confidence 0.60] A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0022 · fairness · confidence 0.85] Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists. (seen 1x in rounds [7]) Conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height, no wall-kick or climb surfaces, exit located above pit. Outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement.

label: analyst
system:
You are the Analyst for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between rounds.
The level is a CSV tile grid ('X' solid, '.' empty, 'S' spawn, 'E' exit; tiles are 32px,
coordinates in feedback are pixels, y grows downward). A human just playtested it.

Respond with ONLY a JSON object in this exact shape:
{
  "diagnosis": "2-4 sentences: what is and isn't working in this level and why",
  "lessons": [
    {
      "lesson": "short, general, reusable design lesson — only a NEW insight",
      "category": "terrain | enemy_placement | difficulty | pacing | fairness | variety | combat | traversal | other",
      "conditions": ["short conditions under which this lesson applies"],
      "outcome": "the observed player or gameplay consequence",
      "confidence": <0.0-1.0 based on strength of evidence>
    }
  ],
  "fun_score": <1-5>,
  "save_level": <true if this level was a standout (4-5) worth keeping for reuse, else false>,
  "save_reason": "one sentence, only if save_level is true"
}

Guidelines:
- Ground everything in evidence: fall locations, completion, and time are stronger
  signals than the comment alone — but take the comment seriously, it's a real human.
- Falls cluster where the level is unfair or too demanding; zero falls plus a fast
  time plus a low rating means the level is under-stimulating.
- Lessons should generalize ("wide pits right after spawn frustrate players"),
  not restate this level's specifics.
- Do not paraphrase a known lesson as a new lesson. Return an empty lessons array
  when the feedback only confirms existing knowledge.

label: object designer
system:
You are the Object Designer for a self-improving 2D platformer. Your job is
to decide whether a new reusable scene object would directly address observed player
feedback, then design its identity and safe data parameters. You never write code.

The engine supports exactly these behavior templates:
- ladder: climbable vertical tiles connecting elevations; parameter climb_speed 1.5-5.0
- bounce: floor spring launching the player upward; parameter launch_speed 8.0-16.0
- hazard: non-solid damaging tile; parameter damage 1-2
- collectible: optional pickup rewarding exploration; parameter value 1-100

Return ONLY this JSON shape:
{
  "new_objects": [
    {
      "name": "short unique display name",
      "description": "what it adds to level design and when it should be used",
      "behavior": "ladder | bounce | hazard | collectible",
      "color": "#RRGGBB",
      "params": {"the behavior's supported parameter": <number>},
      "design_reason": "the feedback or lesson this object addresses"
    }
  ]
}

Add at most one object per round. Return an empty array when the existing catalog can
already address the feedback. Never propose executable code, scripts, physics formulas,
or behavior names outside the supported templates.

label: designer
system:
You are the Designer for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between
rounds to become more fun. Return ONLY one JSON object describing a level plan. Python
will compile the plan into the character grid; never draw or serialize the grid yourself.

Coordinate system: x grows right from 0; y grows down from 0. A solid rectangle
{"x": 10, "y": 12, "width": 20, "height": 2} fills x=10..29 and y=12..13.
The player occupies one empty cell. Gravity pulls down. The player can jump at most
3 columns across and 2 rows up; drops may be any depth.

Return this exact JSON shape:
{
  "width": <integer 250-400>,
  "height": <integer 12-40>,
  "spawn": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "exit": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "solids": [
    {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>, "width": <positive integer>, "height": <positive integer>}
  ],
  "enemies": [
    {"type": <integer 1-9>, "x": <integer>, "y": <integer>}
  ]
}

Hard rules (validated automatically; violations cost a retry):
- Use at most 200 solid rectangles and 24 enemies.
- Every rectangle and entity must be fully inside the level bounds.
- Spawn, exit, and enemy cells must be empty and may not overlap each other.
- Spawn and exit need solid ground directly beneath them.
- Build a continuous reachable route from spawn to exit. Every horizontal gap must be
  at most 3 cells, and upward steps must be at most
  2 rows.
- Use rectangles for floor runs, platforms, stairs, towers, and walls. Split a floor
  into multiple rectangles only where you intentionally want a pit.

Design guidance:
- The camera scrolls with the player. Design a LONG horizontal journey in acts:
  an opening stretch, distinct middle set-pieces (pit fields, staircases, towers,
  descents, enemy gauntlets), and a finale near the exit.
- Directly address the diagnosis. Too hard -> genuinely reduce demands (narrower pits,
  gentler climbs, fewer enemies). Boring -> add variety and encounters.
- Place enemies deliberately: ground enemies (patrollers, turrets) on platforms with
  room to matter; flyers guarding jumps. Space encounters out — clusters read as unfair.
- Apply the accumulated lessons. Keep what the player enjoyed; change the rest.
- Do not place scene objects. A separate Object Designer reads your completed game
  file and applies an independent object-placement pass after this one.
- Falling into a pit respawns the player at S (costs time, not a life).

Return JSON only. Do not include analysis, markdown, code fences, row strings, or ASCII art.

JSON level plan together with potential ladder areas, then choose the candidates that create
intentional vertical routes, alternate paths, recoveries, and pacing changes. Python derived
the potential areas from the private grid and proved each one mechanically valid.

Use the JSON plan's solid rectangles, spawn, exit, enemies, dimensions, and progression to
understand the whole scene. Avoid repetitive evenly spaced ladders; choose a small set whose
locations have distinct gameplay purposes. Return ONLY: {"placements":["L001","L014"]}

Return at most 32 unique candidate IDs exactly as provided. Never invent or modify an ID.
Never include coordinates, reasons, analysis, lessons, prose, markdown, or the game grid.
If no candidate improves the scene, return {"placements":[]}.

COMPLETE PERSISTENT OBJECT-PLACEMENT LESSON HISTORY (follow every lesson as a hard-earned rule):
- [OL-001] Select a ladder run only when every cell from top_y through bottom_y in its x column is '.'. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-002] Choose bottom_y so the cell at (x, bottom_y + 1) is solid X ground. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-003] Use only ladder coordinates inside the supplied grid dimensions; never extrapolate the level width. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-004] Keep ladder count and total expanded ladder tiles within the limits stated in the prompt. (confidence 0.98)

The level is an extremely wide horizontal corridor (hundreds of tiles) with a single repeated macro-structure: a continuous, undulating curve of solid platforms at varying heights but no gaps, ladders, ceilings, or distinct spatial contexts. The player completed 23.8 seconds with zero deaths or falls, killed one enemy, and rated it 2/5, explicitly noting the lack of ladders and platform variance. The design offers traversal without meaningful interaction — no vertical traversal mechanics, no elevation changes that require climbing/descending, and no encounter variety — confirming it functions as a 'walk simulator' along a curved floor.

label: analyst
system:
You are the Analyst for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between rounds.
The level is a CSV tile grid ('X' solid, '.' empty, 'S' spawn, 'E' exit; tiles are 32px,
coordinates in feedback are pixels, y grows downward). A human just playtested it.

Respond with ONLY a JSON object in this exact shape:
{
  "diagnosis": "2-4 sentences: what is and isn't working in this level and why",
  "lessons": [
    {
      "lesson": "short, general, reusable design lesson — only a NEW insight",
      "category": "terrain | enemy_placement | difficulty | pacing | fairness | variety | combat | traversal | other",
      "conditions": ["short conditions under which this lesson applies"],
      "outcome": "the observed player or gameplay consequence",
      "confidence": <0.0-1.0 based on strength of evidence>
    }
  ],
  "fun_score": <1-5>,
  "save_level": <true if this level was a standout (4-5) worth keeping for reuse, else false>,
  "save_reason": "one sentence, only if save_level is true"
}

Guidelines:
- Ground everything in evidence: fall locations, completion, and time are stronger
  signals than the comment alone — but take the comment seriously, it's a real human.
- player_trace is sampled every 750 ms in world-pixel coordinates. Use its timestamped
  path to identify stalls, backtracking, repeated failed approaches, skipped regions,
  traversal speed changes, and where the player disengaged. Do not treat dense nearby
  samples as separate failures; they usually mean the player paused or was blocked.
- Falls cluster where the level is unfair or too demanding; zero falls plus a fast
  time plus a low rating means the level is under-stimulating.
- Lessons should generalize ("wide pits right after spawn frustrate players"),
  not restate this level's specifics.
- Do not paraphrase a known lesson as a new lesson. Return an empty lessons array
  when the feedback only confirms existing knowledge.
response:

  "diagnosis": "The player spawned at (176, 624) on a flat floor and moved right, but repeatedly fell into a deep pit around x=1024–1040, y=548 (two recorded falls). After each fall they respawned at the start and retraced the same horizontal corridor, never reaching the exit. The trace shows long stalls at the spawn and at the far right (x≈1878, y=818) where the player idled for ~20 seconds before the session ended. The level is extremely wide with almost no elevation change, no vertical traversal mechanics, and a deep unclimbable pit that acts as a soft lock. The player rated it 1/5 and commented \"inescapable pit again.\"",
  "lessons": [],
  "fun_score": 1,
  "save_level": false,
  "save_reason": ""
}

label: object designer
system:
You are the Object Designer for a self-improving 2D platformer. Your job is
to decide whether a new reusable scene object would directly address observed player
feedback, then design its identity and safe data parameters. You never write code.

The engine supports exactly these behavior templates:
- ladder: climbable vertical tiles connecting elevations; parameter climb_speed 1.5-5.0
- bounce: floor spring launching the player upward; parameter launch_speed 8.0-16.0
- hazard: non-solid damaging tile; parameter damage 1-2
- collectible: optional pickup rewarding exploration; parameter value 1-100

Return ONLY this JSON shape:
{
  "new_objects": [
    {
      "name": "short unique display name",
      "description": "what it adds to level design and when it should be used",
      "behavior": "ladder | bounce | hazard | collectible",
      "color": "#RRGGBB",
      "params": {"the behavior's supported parameter": <number>},
      "design_reason": "the feedback or lesson this object addresses"
    }
  ]
}

Add at most one object per round. Return an empty array when the existing catalog can
already address the feedback. Never propose executable code, scripts, physics formulas,
or behavior names outside the supported templates.

label: designer
system:
You are the Designer for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between
rounds to become more fun. Return ONLY one JSON object describing a level plan. Python
will compile the plan into the character grid; never draw or serialize the grid yourself.

Coordinate system: x grows right from 0; y grows down from 0. A solid rectangle
{"x": 10, "y": 12, "width": 20, "height": 2} fills x=10..29 and y=12..13.
The player occupies one empty cell. Gravity pulls down. The player can jump at most
3 columns across and 2 rows up; drops may be any depth.

Return this exact JSON shape:
{
  "width": <integer 250-400>,
  "height": <integer 12-40>,
  "spawn": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "exit": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "solids": [
    {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>, "width": <positive integer>, "height": <positive integer>}
  ],
  "enemies": [
    {"type": <integer 1-9>, "x": <integer>, "y": <integer>}
  ]
}

Hard rules (validated automatically; violations cost a retry):
- Use at most 200 solid rectangles and 24 enemies.
- Every rectangle and entity must be fully inside the level bounds.
- Spawn, exit, and enemy cells must be empty and may not overlap each other.
- Spawn and exit need solid ground directly beneath them.
- Build a continuous reachable route from spawn to exit. Every horizontal gap must be
  at most 3 cells, and upward steps must be at most
  2 rows.
- Use rectangles for floor runs, platforms, stairs, towers, and walls. Split a floor
  into multiple rectangles only where you intentionally want a pit.

Design guidance:
- The camera scrolls with the player. Design a LONG horizontal journey in acts:
  an opening stretch, distinct middle set-pieces (pit fields, staircases, towers,
  descents, enemy gauntlets), and a finale near the exit.
- Directly address the diagnosis. Too hard -> genuinely reduce demands (narrower pits,
  gentler climbs, fewer enemies). Boring -> add variety and encounters.
- Place enemies deliberately: ground enemies (patrollers, turrets) on platforms with
  room to matter; flyers guarding jumps. Space encounters out — clusters read as unfair.
- Apply the accumulated lessons. Keep what the player enjoyed; change the rest.
- Do not place scene objects. A separate Object Designer reads your completed game
  file and applies an independent object-placement pass after this one.
- Falling into a pit respawns the player at S (costs time, not a life).

Return JSON only. Do not include analysis, markdown, code fences, row strings, or ASCII art.

ANALYST DIAGNOSIS:
The player spawned at (176, 624) on a flat floor and moved right, but repeatedly fell into a deep pit around x=1024–1040, y=548 (two recorded falls). After each fall they respawned at the start and retraced the same horizontal corridor, never reaching the exit. The trace shows long stalls at the spawn and at the far right (x≈1878, y=818) where the player idled for ~20 seconds before the session ended. The level is extremely wide with almost no elevation change, no vertical traversal mechanics, and a deep unclimbable pit that acts as a soft lock. The player rated it 1/5 and commented "inescapable pit again."

THE PLAYER'S OWN WORDS (verbatim — honor explicit requests where the format allows):
"inescapable pit again"

FUN SCORE: 1/5

ACCUMULATED LESSONS:
- [lesson_0001 · pacing · confidence 0.85] A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun. (seen 5x in rounds [1, 3]) Conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction, zero enemies or hazards present, exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles, fast completion time with zero deaths/falls. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer.
- [lesson_0023 · pacing · confidence 0.90] Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. (seen 3x in rounds [8, 9, 12]) Conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles), no significant elevation changes over long stretches, no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics, single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density, player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation, level width greatly exceeds height, repeating enemy/platform patterns, one jump type dominates traversal, repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. Outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation.
- [lesson_0020 · other · confidence 0.60] Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0022 · fairness · confidence 0.85] Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists. (seen 1x in rounds [7]) Conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height, no wall-kick or climb surfaces, exit located above pit. Outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement.
- [lesson_0010 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Stationary shooters placed on the same horizontal plane as the player's movement path become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening. (seen 3x in rounds [4])
- [lesson_0003 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.80] Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide. (seen 3x in rounds [2, 5]) Conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending), enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane, aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes.
- [lesson_0004 · traversal · confidence 0.60] Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0006 · fairness · confidence 0.60] An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0021 · traversal · confidence 0.60] A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0015 · terrain · confidence 0.80] Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation. (seen 3x in rounds [5]) Conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure, no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints), continuous height gradient without traversal variety. Outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry.

label: analyst
system:
You are the Analyst for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between rounds.

{

  "lessons": [
    {
      "lesson": "short, general, reusable design lesson — only a NEW insight",
      "category": "terrain | enemy_placement | difficulty | pacing | fairness | variety | combat | traversal | other",
      "conditions": ["short conditions under which this lesson applies"],
      "outcome": "the observed player or gameplay consequence",

  "save_level": <true if this level was a standout (4-5) worth keeping for reuse, else false>,

- Ground everything in evidence: fall locations, completion, and time are stronger
  signals than the comment alone — but take the comment seriously, it's a real human.

  traversal speed changes, and where the player disengaged. Do not treat dense nearby
  samples as separate failures; they usually mean the player paused or was blocked.

  time plus a low rating means the level is under-stimulating.
- Lessons should generalize ("wide pits right after spawn frustrate players"),
  not restate this level's specifics.
- Do not paraphrase a known lesson as a new lesson. Return an empty lessons array
  when the feedback only confirms existing knowledge.

label: designer
system:
You are the Designer for a 2D tile platformer that redesigns itself between
rounds to become more fun. Return ONLY one JSON object describing a level plan. Python
will compile the plan into the character grid; never draw or serialize the grid yourself.

Coordinate system: x grows right from 0; y grows down from 0. A solid rectangle
{"x": 10, "y": 12, "width": 20, "height": 2} fills x=10..29 and y=12..13.
The player occupies one empty cell. Gravity pulls down. The player can jump at most
3 columns across and 2 rows up; drops may be any depth.

Return this exact JSON shape:
{
  "width": <integer 250-400>,
  "height": <integer 12-40>,
  "spawn": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "exit": {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>},
  "solids": [
    {"x": <integer>, "y": <integer>, "width": <positive integer>, "height": <positive integer>}
  ],
  "enemies": [
    {"type": <integer 1-9>, "x": <integer>, "y": <integer>}
  ]
}

Hard rules (validated automatically; violations cost a retry):
- Use at most 200 solid rectangles and 24 enemies.
- Every rectangle and entity must be fully inside the level bounds.
- Spawn, exit, and enemy cells must be empty and may not overlap each other.
- Spawn and exit need solid ground directly beneath them.
- Build a continuous reachable route from spawn to exit. Every horizontal gap must be
  at most 3 cells, and upward steps must be at most
  2 rows.
- Use rectangles for floor runs, platforms, stairs, towers, and walls. Split a floor
  into multiple rectangles only where you intentionally want a pit.

Design guidance:
- The camera scrolls with the player. Design a LONG horizontal journey in acts:
  an opening stretch, distinct middle set-pieces (pit fields, staircases, towers,
  descents, enemy gauntlets), and a finale near the exit.
- Directly address the diagnosis. Too hard -> genuinely reduce demands (narrower pits,
  gentler climbs, fewer enemies). Boring -> add variety and encounters.
- Place enemies deliberately: ground enemies (patrollers, turrets) on platforms with
  room to matter; flyers guarding jumps. Space encounters out — clusters read as unfair.
- Apply the accumulated lessons. Keep what the player enjoyed; change the rest.
- Do not place scene objects. A separate Object Designer reads your completed game
  file and applies an independent object-placement pass after this one.
- Falling into a pit respawns the player at S (costs time, not a life).

Return JSON only. Do not include analysis, markdown, code fences, row strings, or ASCII art.

ANALYST DIAGNOSIS:
The player started at the bottom-left spawn, moved right along the floor, then ascended a tall vertical shaft via ladders (reaching y≈483 at t=5.2s), but fell back down to the mid-level corridor around y=754 and spent the remaining ~23 seconds pacing horizontally without finding a way back up or forward to the exit. The level's vertical variety and ladder density were engaging initially, but the long climb lacked mid-shaft rest points or clear wayfinding, causing a fall that reset progress without a recovery path. The player enjoyed the variance but explicitly criticized ladder design on long walls.

THE PLAYER'S OWN WORDS (verbatim — honor explicit requests where the format allows):
"Felt good! i like the variance, needs better ladder design on long walls"

FUN SCORE: 3/5

ACCUMULATED LESSONS:
- [lesson_0017 · traversal · confidence 0.85] Long uninterrupted ladder climbs without rest platforms or horizontal traversal options cause player fatigue and falls, turning vertical traversal into a punishment loop rather than a challenge. (seen 1x in rounds [17]) Conditions: vertical shaft exceeds ~10 tiles height, ladders are the sole traversal method, no horizontal ledges, platforms, or branching paths along the climb. Outcome: player falls mid-climb, loses significant progress, disengages from vertical section.
- [lesson_0014 · pacing · confidence 0.78] Vertical challenge clusters near the level end amplify perceived difficulty spikes — players who traverse long horizontal stretches expect the finale to test mastery, not introduce new traversal demands without checkpoints. (seen 1x in rounds [17]) Conditions: long horizontal traversal precedes final vertical section, no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones before finale, final section requires repeated precise climbs. Outcome: player falls late, respawns to start, repeats 100+ seconds of content, rates level 3/5 despite enjoying variety.
- [lesson_0023 · pacing · confidence 0.90] Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. (seen 3x in rounds [8, 9, 12]) Conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles), no significant elevation changes over long stretches, no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics, single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density, player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation, level width greatly exceeds height, repeating enemy/platform patterns, one jump type dominates traversal, repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. Outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation.
- [lesson_0006 · fairness · confidence 0.60] An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0022 · fairness · confidence 0.85] Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists. (seen 1x in rounds [7]) Conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height, no wall-kick or climb surfaces, exit located above pit. Outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement.
- [lesson_0001 · pacing · confidence 0.85] A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun. (seen 5x in rounds [1, 3]) Conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction, zero enemies or hazards present, exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles, fast completion time with zero deaths/falls. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer.
- [lesson_0003 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.80] Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide. (seen 3x in rounds [2, 5]) Conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending), enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane, aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes.
- [lesson_0020 · other · confidence 0.60] Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0004 · traversal · confidence 0.60] Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0015 · terrain · confidence 0.80] Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation. (seen 3x in rounds [5]) Conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure, no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints), continuous height gradient without traversal variety. Outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry.
- [lesson_0005 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Flying enemies placed above the player's maximum projectile arc create unfair, uncounterable threats that frustrate rather than challenge. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0021 · traversal · confidence 0.60] A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it. (seen 1x in rounds [6])

REUSABLE LIBRARY:
- [levels] level_001_r002: High rating with strong combat engagement (8 kills, real risk from falls) shows enemy/hazard density works well as a baseline layer to build verticality onto.

Design the next level.

ANALYST DIAGNOSIS:
The player spent ~48 seconds holding right on a flat floor at y=882 with zero vertical traversal, zero deaths, and zero falls, killing only 4 enemies along a 7000+ pixel horizontal stretch. The trace shows a single brief elevation change around x=1779–2464 (a small hill) before returning to the same flat baseline for the remaining 5000+ pixels. The exit at row 3 (~96px) is vertically disconnected from the spawn floor at row 26 (~832px) with no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical pathways visible in the CSV. The player never reached the exit, never fell, and disengaged at x≈7388 after prolonged monotonous walking. This confirms the level is a pure horizontal grind with no structural variety or verticality.

THE PLAYER'S OWN WORDS (verbatim — honor explicit requests where the format allows):
"idk just redo it"

FUN SCORE: 1/5

ACCUMULATED LESSONS:
- [lesson_0023 · pacing · confidence 0.90] Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. (seen 3x in rounds [8, 9, 12]) Conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles), no significant elevation changes over long stretches, no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics, single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density, player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation, level width greatly exceeds height, repeating enemy/platform patterns, one jump type dominates traversal, repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles. Outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation.
- [lesson_0001 · pacing · confidence 0.85] A level that can be completed quickly with minimal player input (holding one direction, zero deaths/falls, no obstacles or enemies) fails to engage players regardless of polish — traversal alone without meaningful interaction is insufficient for fun. (seen 5x in rounds [1, 3]) Conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction, zero enemies or hazards present, exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles, fast completion time with zero deaths/falls. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer.
- [lesson_0004 · traversal · confidence 0.60] Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0003 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.80] Enemy density and variety cannot compensate for flat terrain lacking vertical traversal — engaging combat requires diverse spatial contexts (elevation changes, cover, chokepoints) that flat levels cannot provide. (seen 3x in rounds [2, 5]) Conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending), enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane, aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc. Outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes.
- [lesson_0014 · pacing · confidence 0.78] Vertical challenge clusters near the level end amplify perceived difficulty spikes — players who traverse long horizontal stretches expect the finale to test mastery, not introduce new traversal demands without checkpoints. (seen 1x in rounds [17]) Conditions: long horizontal traversal precedes final vertical section, no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones before finale, final section requires repeated precise climbs. Outcome: player falls late, respawns to start, repeats 100+ seconds of content, rates level 3/5 despite enjoying variety.
- [lesson_0010 · enemy_placement · confidence 0.60] Stationary shooters placed on the same horizontal plane as the player's movement path become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening. (seen 3x in rounds [4])
- [lesson_0017 · traversal · confidence 0.85] Long uninterrupted ladder climbs without rest platforms or horizontal traversal options cause player fatigue and falls, turning vertical traversal into a punishment loop rather than a challenge. (seen 1x in rounds [17]) Conditions: vertical shaft exceeds ~10 tiles height, ladders are the sole traversal method, no horizontal ledges, platforms, or branching paths along the climb. Outcome: player falls mid-climb, loses significant progress, disengages from vertical section.
- [lesson_0020 · other · confidence 0.60] Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0021 · traversal · confidence 0.60] A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it. (seen 1x in rounds [6])
- [lesson_0015 · terrain · confidence 0.80] Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation. (seen 3x in rounds [5]) Conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure, no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints), continuous height gradient without traversal variety. Outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry.
- [lesson_0006 · fairness · confidence 0.60] An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork. (seen 1x in rounds [2])
- [lesson_0022 · fairness · confidence 0.85] Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists. (seen 1x in rounds [7]) Conditions: pit depth exceeds max jump height, no wall-kick or climb surfaces, exit located above pit. Outcome: player falls, cannot recover, rates level poorly despite earlier engagement.

REUSABLE LIBRARY:
- [levels] level_001_r002: High rating with strong combat engagement (8 kills, real risk from falls) shows enemy/hazard density works well as a baseline layer to build verticality onto.

label: object placement designer
system:
You are an expert ladder placement designer. Study the generated
JSON level plan together with potential ladder areas, then choose the candidates that create
intentional vertical routes, alternate paths, recoveries, and pacing changes. Python derived
the potential areas from the private grid and proved each one mechanically valid by rebuilding
the player's navigation graph with that ladder present.

Use the JSON plan's solid rectangles, spawn, exit, enemies, dimensions, and progression to
understand the whole scene. Avoid repetitive evenly spaced ladders; choose a small set whose
locations have distinct gameplay purposes. Prefer priority=critical_access prospects because
they unlock standable regions that traversal validation cannot reach without a ladder. Rank
critical prospects by inaccessible_coverage and unlocked_standable_cells. Candidates with the same unlocked_region_id
serve the same inaccessible region, so select at most one of them. Treat alternate_route
prospects as optional shortcuts, not mandatory decoration. Do not add a ladder merely because
a platform exists; every selection should add useful reachability or a deliberate alternate route.
Return ONLY: {"placements":["L001","L014"]}

Return at most 32 unique candidate IDs exactly as provided. Never invent or modify an ID.
Never include coordinates, reasons, analysis, lessons, prose, markdown, or the game grid.
If no candidate improves the scene, return {"placements":[]}.

COMPLETE PERSISTENT OBJECT-PLACEMENT LESSON HISTORY (follow every lesson as a hard-earned rule):
- [OL-001] Select a ladder run only when every cell from top_y through bottom_y in its x column is '.'. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-002] Choose bottom_y so the cell at (x, bottom_y + 1) is solid X ground. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-003] Use only ladder coordinates inside the supplied grid dimensions; never extrapolate the level width. (confidence 0.98)
- [OL-004] Keep ladder count and total expanded ladder tiles within the limits stated in the prompt. (confidence 0.98)
